title: Pair Deduction Printer
prompt_md:
## Goal
Use class template argument deduction (CTAD) to create `std::pair` objects without spelling out their template arguments, then print them.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer and a double, space-separated
- Line 2: two integers, space-separated

## Output
Three lines, each `<first> <second>`:
- the first pair (int, double)
- the second pair (int, int)
- a third pair built from the first element of pair 1 and the first element of pair 2

## Constraints
- Create all three pairs with CTAD -- write `std::pair{ ... }` with no explicit `<...>` template arguments, and do not use `auto` for the pair declarations.

test_cases.label: Sample -- basic int and double pair

title: Labeled Value with Deduction Guide
prompt_md:
## Goal
Write a struct template `Labeled` that pairs a label with a value of any type, and add a deduction guide so that constructing it from a string literal stores the label as a `std::string` (not a `const char*`, which could not be printed safely later). Then rely on CTAD plus your guide to build and print labeled values.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer (the value to label `count`)
- Line 2: a double (the value to label `price`)

## Output
Two lines:
```
[count] <integer>
[price] <double>
```

## Constraints
- `Labeled<T>` has members `std::string label` and `T value`, and no user-declared constructor (rely on aggregate initialization).
- Provide a deduction guide mapping `(const char*, T)` to `Labeled<T>`.
- Construct each object from a string-literal label and the input value using CTAD -- no explicit template arguments. Do not use `std::pair`.
